1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 1989 1988 1987 1986 1985 1984 Skip to sidebar Modeling Timed Concurrent Systems Xiaojun Liu, Eleftherios Matsikoudis, and Edward A. Lee in Proceedings of the 17th International Conference on Concurrency Th

he 17th International Conference on Concurrency Theory (CONCUR) , Bonn, Germany, August 27-30, 2006. Prepublished version Published version ABSTRACT Timed concurrent systems are widely used in concurrent and distributed real-time software, modeling o

ent and distributed real-time software, modeling of hybrid systems, design of hardware systems (using hardware description languages), discrete-event simulation, and modeling of communication networks. They consist of concurrent components that commu

. They consist of concurrent components that communicate using timed signals, that is, sets of (semantically) time-stamped events. The denotational semantics of such systems is traditionally formulated in a metric space, wherein causal components are

d in a metric space, wherein causal components are modeled as contracting functions. We show that this formulation excessively restricts the models of time that can be used. In particular, it cannot handle super-dense time, commonly used in hardware

andle super-dense time, commonly used in hardware description languages and hybrid systems modeling, finite time lines, and time with no origin. Moreover, if we admit continuoustime and mixed signals (essential for hybrid systems modeling) or certain

(essential for hybrid systems modeling) or certain Zeno signals, then causality is no longer equivalent to its formalization in terms of contracting functions. In this paper, we offer an alternative semantic framework using a generalized ultrametric
emantic framework using a generalized ultrametric that overcomes these limitations. Contact Update Help Update Copyright © 1999 - UC Regents; all rights reserved.
